page_content:
# Passage: 6
The fine regulation of mitochondrial functions in response to fluctuations in oxygen availability is essential for metabolic homeostasis. Under normal oxygen levels (normoxia), the majority of the cell’s energy is generated through aerobic respiration. In contrast, under low oxygen conditions (hypoxia), cells increase their glycolytic energy production, and oxidative phosphorylation is attenuated in order to reduce oxygen consumption.  
Studies have shown that the activity of mitochondrial Complex I is regulated by oxygen availability. Researchers hypothesized that a subunit of Complex I (NDU) is involved in hypoxia-induced mitochondrial reprogramming and oxygen consumption regulation.  
Researchers measured the expression level of two proteins (phosphoglucose isomerase and NDU) under conditions of normoxia (Nx) and hypoxia (Hx) (figures 1 and 2). They also generated NDU-deficient cells (NDU-KO) and compared their mitochondrial functions to wild-type (WT) cells (Figure 3).  
BB-8-1-201BB_88036-00P1.gif  
Figure 1The effects of hypoxia on the expression level of phosphoglucose isomerase by RT-PCR
BB-8-1-201BB_88036-00P2.gif  
Figure 2The effects of hypoxia on the expression of NDU by (A) RT-PCR and (B) Western blot analysis
BB-8-1-201BB_88036-00P3.gif  
Figure 3The effects of hypoxia on the mitochondrial functions (A) oxygen consumption, (B) Complex I activity, and (C) Complex IV activity in WT and NDU-KO cells (Note: * indicates p < 0.05.)
